One thing that does set Bucephalandra apart from Anubias is that it does prefer soft to moderate hardness compared the medium to high hardness. This makes buce a great choice for softwater tanks.

If you are only ordering plants, you are eligible for a shipping rate of $19.99. Please include a note or email requesting a partial shipping refund when you order.

*Plants may contain snails and/or snail eggs.

***** Please read our SHIPPING AND HANDLING FAQ before ordering. It contains important information about order fulfillment time, shipping speed, etc. *****

products.body_html:
Remarks: Bucephalandra green wavy is a great introduction to keeping Bucephalandra species as it is a faster growing variant and is fairly hardy. As the name implies, the leaves of this buce are a vibrant green and get a very unique looking wavey edge to its leaves. This interesting plant can add some great texture variation to just about any planted tank.

products.title: Bucephalandra Brownie Ghost 'Ulu Kapus'
products.body_html:
Remarks: Bucephalandra brownie ghost 'Ulu Kapus' is an uncommon and breath-taking variant of Bucephalandra. The tops of its smooth leaves are a green so deep it is nearly black and covered in tiny white speckles. This combined with the iridescence they get in certain lighting gives an appearance almost like the night sky. Beneath these gorgeous leaves, they are a pleasant light green and connect to a red stem to finish off this incredible plant. It goes without saying that Bucephalandra brownie ghost 'Ulu Kapus' makes its slightly more sensitive disposition compared to other buce worth the effort to keep it healthy.

  Bucephalandra are similar to Anubias in that they are epiphytic meaning that they grow on structures such as wood, rocks, or decor rather than into the soil. products.title: Fenestrata Cichlid "Las Playas"
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The fenestrata cichlid is an uncommon relative of the popular synspilum cichlid with the two looking very similar. Both display similar colors such as a typically pink or red head and a green or blue body. However, what sets a fenestrata cichlid apart from a synspilum are the faint blue and red speckles on the face that are more apparent in juveniles, the black bar on their lateral line being more solid and extending to the pectoral fin rather than just the mid body, and their higher propensity toward having varied colors and patterns among individuals. 

  In the wild, the fenestrata cichlid can only be found in slow-moving rivers and lakes between the Papaloapán and Chachalacas rivers in western Mexico. These areas have sandy or muddy bottoms with large areas lush with aquatic plants that these fish graze on. While fenestrata cichlids are most herbivorous, they will occasionally also feed on insects and small crustaceans. This reflects the diet they should be provided in captivity, heavy in vegetable based foods and low in protein to prevent bloat. Frequent water changes and excellent filtration are also recommended as they come from areas with clear, pristine water.

  Choosing tankmates for the fenestrata cichlid can be challenging as they are very large and boisterous fish that can be extremely territorial when spawning and already require at least a 90 gallon aquarium for just a pair. When kept in a community setting, they will need a tank that is at least 150 gallons with tons of structure to provide territory space and breaks in the line of sight. The best options for possible tankmates are other Central American cichlids that share their size and temperament and do not belong to the Vieja genus to prevent hybridization. Whether kept in a pair or in a Central American community setting, the fenestrata cichlid is a very fascinating fish with their beautiful colors, bold personalities, and impressive size.

products.title: Opae Ula Shrimp
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Opae ula, also known as Hawaiian red shrimp or volcano shrimp, are incredible examples of how adaptable some animals can be. These tiny, bright red shrimp live only in brackish pools on the Hawaiian Islands, most commonly in volcanic anchialine pools which gives them their other common name of volcano shrimp. These shallow, brackish pools have forced this species to become incredibly hardy as salinity and temperature levels fluctuate with with the tides. These levels can range anywhere from a temperature in the high 30s to the low 90s (in  Fahrenheit) and salinity levels from 1.000 specific gravity all the way to 1.026 specific gravity, that is anywhere from completely fresh water to full saltwater! Not only that, wild shrimps also will sometimes have to go long periods of time without food resulting in them being able to survive on very little for years on end.

  While opae ula can survive these incredible extremes and through times of heavily fouled water and scarce food, this does not mean they will thrive in improper conditions in the home aquarium forever. Their hardiness has resulted in it being popular to keep these shrimps in "ecospheres" which are completely enclosed spheres with the belief they will be fully self-sustaining systems. The reality is that these systems, while they may last for a few years, are not perfect systems and will eventually crash long before the full potential of the opae ula's lifespan is carried out as they slowly die from a mixture of suffocation, starvation, and poisoning from fouling water.

  Instead, these shrimps should be kept in a brackish water set up with a salinity between 1.008 - 1.016 specific gravity that has been given time to cycle and accumulate at least a little bit of algae and biofilm for them to feed on. We keep our colonies at a salinity of about 1.010 - 1.012 specific gravity. As these shrimps are very hardy and low maintenance, they can typically go months without a water change. Anything is on the menu as well so they can be fed a variety of foods for aquatics along with fresh vegetables like zucchini. Due to the fact that they are so small and require brackish conditions, most tankmates are advised against though some options are nerite snails and neon blue eye rainbowfish, however these fish should only be kept with well established colonies as they will occasionally eat shrimplets. Provided the right conditions opae ula can be excellent pets with their low maintenance, bright red colors, and a lifespan of up to 20 years in captivity.

products.title: L400 Decorated Zebra Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The L400 decorated zebra pleco is another Hypancistrus species from the Rio Xingu in Brazil, particularly the lower Rio Xingu. Like many others in their genus, they have a mostly white body covered in black or dark gray striations, stripes and spots. There is a noticably high level of pattern variation among individuals as each fish is unique in the amount of each pattern type they will show. For example, some L400 decorated zebra plecos completely lack spots, while others have more spots than stripes. To make them even more unique and fascinating, these patterns frequently change throughout the fish's lifespan. This can make them very interesting to watch grow and see how they change over time.

  Recently in 2025, a taxonomic study was conducted on the L400 decorated zebra pleco along with a number of other similar looking Hypancistrus. This study determined that the plecos under the L-numbers L333, L66, L236, L399 and L400 were all just different variations/localities of the same highly variable new species. This new species name was deemed Hypancistrus seideli. They were named after professional aquarist, Ingo Seidel, who has been a great influence within the hobby and the scientific realm in regards to Loricariids. He especially made an impact in regards to Hypancistrus species so it is very fitting that he get these amazing little catfish named in honor of him. You can read more on this study by clicking HERE.

  The L400 decorated zebra pleco is a typical Hypancistrus in regards to their care in captivity. They come from rocky areas of pristine, heavily oxygenated, warm, soft, and acidic water and will gradually decline in health in the home aquarium if these conditions are not replicated. Using a high quality filter with a high flow rate along with a heater and frequent water changes will help keep these plecos happy and healthy. Like all known Hypancistrus, the L400 decorated zebra pleco is a carnivore so they must be fed a varied diet high in protein. Protein rich gel foods like Repashy, bloodworms, and sinking wafers are good choices to feed to meet their dietary needs. 

  To reduce territorial fights and help the L400 decorated zebra pleco feel comfortable in the aquarium, plenty of snug, rocky caves should be provided for them to hide in. These caves are also where these fish will spawn if all other conditions are just right. They can be a very exciting breeding project as they are such a unique and beautiful catfish. An important thing to note, however, is that it is not wise to house the L400 decorated zebra pleco with other similar Hypancistrus as they can hybridize with it being near impossible to tell pure fish from hybrids. We encourage anyone who sets out to keep these fish make an effort to ensure captive populations remain pure to preserve this species in light of dam construction in Brazil destroying their natural habitat.

products.title: Black Oto
products.body_html:
Remarks: The black oto is a tiny catfish native to the Rio Piribebuy from which it gets the second part of its scientific name (Otothyropsis piribebuy). They bear a number of resemblances to the popular common oto both in structure, behavior, and care, but they are not even in the same genus. Like the common oto, the black oto is a very small, peaceful fish that rarely exceeds an inch in length and lives in groups of hundreds in the wild. Like all otos, they have a flat belly and a sucker-like mouth to help them stick to surfaces where they graze on biofilm and algae. They differ from common otos in that they have a more stream-lined body, a less flattened head, smaller eyes, and a different color pattern. The black oto, true to its name, is sometimes totally black or at least dark gray or brown but it can sometimes present a mottled pattern

  As this species is very social, in captivity they should never be kept in groups smaller than at least 6-8 to help them feel secure. Providing plenty of hiding spaces such as with live plants and driftwood will further encourage them to explore their surroundings. Black otos do not have too many defenses and are rather timid so aggressive tankmates or those large enough to swallow them should be avoided. They also require high levels of dissolved oxygen and do not tolerate poor water quality so heavy filtration and frequent water changes are a must. If a tank has high flow, it can be very entertaining to watch them play in the current.

  Like most species of oto catfish, black otos make great fish for algae management, but what comes with their amazing ability to clean up algae is a very high metabolism requiring that they be able to graze 24/7. For this reason, they should not be added to a tank that does not yet have algae present and should be fed many frequent, small meals. Vegetables such as zucchini and green beans can be an excellent treat to ensure these little catfish remain happy and healthy.

products.title: Towuti Gold Spotted Rabbit Snail
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Towuti gold spotted rabbit snail is one of the many diverse species of snail belonging to the genus Tylomenia and one of the handful of color variants of the Towuti rabbit snail. They come from the mountain lakes and rivers in Sulawesi, Indonesia where they burrow through the soft sand or mud looking for decaying plant matter and detritus to eat. Being omnivores, they are rather opportunistic and will eat most foods offered to them. Some Tylomenia species might eat plants, but this species seems to actually completely ignore plants except for when they clean algae off them.

  While many snails in the hobby prefer somewhat cooler water, the Towuti Rabbit Snails actually prefer to be kept in warmer water with the sweet spot sitting at about 82 degrees. Rabbit snails are named because of their movement strategy: They launch their head (or foot) forward, grabs hold of the substrate or glass, and pulls its long shell behind it. The name also comes from their long, droopy antennae that some fish may find irresistible so care should be taken when choosing tank mates to avoid nipping. Towuti Rabbit Snails have shells that vary in color and pattern, being anywhere from ivory to dark brown and a body that is an elegant black with gold speckles. To keep their beautiful shells intact, these snails are best kept in harder, more alkaline water with sources of minerals and calcium to feed on such as empty shells, cleaned egg shells, or tourmaline balls. If well cared for, Towuti Rabbit Snails make an excellent member of your tank's clean up crew.

products.title: L475 Nhamunda Zebra Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Nhamunda zebra pleco is one of many look-a-like species within the Hypancistrus genus. It can be rather difficult to tell them apart from closely related species such as the Colombian Zebra Pleco and the Chocolate Zebra pleco. To make things even more complicated identification-wise, it is common for these plecos to hybridize with other species within their natural range in the Rio Nhamunda. All of these species display a pattern of dark gray, black, or brown bodies covered in a varied pattern of white or off-white stripes resulting in each fish having a unique pattern. Combined with their small size, they are typically a rather desirable species for the home aquarium.

  Care for the Nhamunda zebra pleco is like most other Hypancistrus where they prefer to be kept in groups otherwise they will feel unsafe. They also are mainly carnivorous so a diet rich in protein but with the occasional vegetable is needed to keep them healthy. As they are found in the same waters as wild discus, they differ from most Loricariids in that they require very warm water rather than cooler temperatures. They also require very low nitrate levels like discus making them an excellent tankmate for them and many other species that prefer warm, soft, acidic, and tannin-stained conditions such as ram cichlids. It should be noted when choosing tankmates that the Nhamunda zebra pleco can be quite shy so overly boisterous and aggressive species should be avoided and care should be taken to ensure they get enough to eat as they are easily outcompeted.

products.title: Blue Cheek Apisto
products.body_html:
Remarks: Apistogramma eunotus, also known as the blue cheek apisto, is a small, fairly peaceful, and somewhat hardy species of dwarf cichlid. They rarely exceed 2.5" in length and outside of the expected territorial behavior toward conspecifics, are fairly timid and non-combative for an Apistogramma. Juvenile fish tend to be totally gray or silver with a black spot at the base of the caudal fin and females display the typical yellow bodies with black markings when mature and in breeding dress. Mature and dominant males have very attractive coloration with blue bodies, anal fins, and ventral fins accented by orange or yellow eyes, caudal fins, and dorsal fins. They also have a spot of red at the base of their pectoral fins and metallic blue gill plates which gives them their common name of the blue cheek apisto. Along with this brilliant color, dominant males tend to also have elegant extensions on the back of their dorsal fins and sometimes their anal fins.

  While the blue cheek apisto is notably hardy and adaptable for an Apistogramma, they do best in captivity when their natural habitat is mimicked. In the wild, they inhabit small, shallow, soft, acidic, and tannin-stained streams and tributaries of the Rio Ucayali in central Peru all the way down to the Japura drainage in Brazil. These areas have plenty of fallen branches and botanicals littering the substrate of soft sand. Among these, the blue cheek apisto seeks out small caves in which pairs will spawn. Replicating these conditions within the aquarium as well as providing peaceful dither fish such as small pencilfish or tetras, plenty of small live foods, and live plants will encourage this species to spawn. An established pair will work together to guard their eggs and newborn fry until the babies are large enough to fend for themselves.

  As they are a more retiring species, it is not recommended to keep the blue cheek apisto with aggressive fish or other species of Apistogramma. Doing such can cause them to hide constantly and possibly will result in injuries or even deaths. While this does limit what species they can be housed with in regards to larger and more robust fish, it does make them a great choice for other smaller, more timid fish such as hatchetfish, pencilfish, smaller rasboras, and smaller tetras. With their small size, vibrant colors, and hardy nature, the blue cheek apisto can be a great addition to smaller, more peaceful community aquariums.

products.title: Blushing Adolfo's Hoplisoma
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The blushing Adolfo's hoplisoma bears a close resemblance to the traditional Adolfo's hoplisoma (Hoplisoma adolfoi) along with a few other Corydoradinae from the Rio Negro. In fact, they look exactly the same as the traditional Adolfo's hoplisoma with the only visual difference being that the blushing Adolfo's hoplisoma has a clear gill plate revealing the dark red gills underneath making it look like the fish is blushing. Just like their look-a-like, the rest of their appearance consists of a white body, a bright orange spot between their dorsal fin and their eyes, a thick black stripe going down their back and extending up into the dorsal fin, and another black band that runs across their eyes like a mask.

  Blushing Adolfo’s hoplisoma naturally live in extremely soft, acidic waters rich with tannins and will do best in captivity when these conditions are replicated. This can be achieved through the use of an RODI machine as well as the addition of wood and botanicals such as Indian almond leaves. It was previously classified as Corydoras sp. (C121), but was reclassified as Hoplisoma sp. (C121) upon publication of a phylogenomic analysis in 2024 that reclassified many species in the Corydoradinae subfamily.

  Corydoradinae are small, peaceful catfish that are active shoalers and will stay near conspecifics, making for a lovely sight. They feed by searching through sand with their barbels, making such a substrate necessary for their long-term health. Gravel substrates tend to be abrasive and damage the barbels, leading to infections and an inability to find food.

  While these friendly catfish are willing to swim with other species of Corydoradinae different from their own, this does not provide the same social need as being kept with members of their own species. Because of this, it is highly recommended to keep at least six of each species of Corydoradinae present in the aquarium and only mix species if one has the space to keep at least six of each.

  Corydoradinae are often looked at as "scavengers," but they should be treated in a similar fashion as the rest of your fish. They are omnivores and require a mixed diet. Sinking prepared foods are readily accepted, as are frozen and live foods such as brine shrimp, blackworms, and bloodworms.

products.title: LDA025 Pitbull Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The pitbull pleco is a fascinating dwarf species of Loricariid known from clear streams and rivers in eastern Brazil. For many years they were an important staple for Brazilian exporters and breeders as they were heavily in demand for the aquarium hobby similar to the role held by otocinclus today. When heavy export restraints were placed on Brazilian species, the market all but fizzled out save for the few already existing in captivity on other continents. This lasted for about 10 years causing the pitbull pleco to essentially disappear from the hobby. However, this fish is now making a big comeback with Brazil exports being reopened and heavy breeding efforts being undertaken in places such as Indonesia making them a bit more affordable and available.

  This oddball of a tiny catfish attained its popularity in the past for a reason. They were known for their voracious appetite for algae, peaceful disposition, social proclivities, and fairly high levels of activity. The pitbull pleco lives in groups of hundreds in the wild so they prefer to be kept in groups of at least 3-6 in captivity with more always being better. The larger the group, the more brave they will be and the more likely you will see fascinating interactions between individuals as they establish their pecking order and males attempt to court females. While they will feed on algae and biofilm on just about any surface, the pitbull pleco does prefer to spend most of its time on the bottom of the tank where it will occasionally bury itself to rest and hide if the substrate allows for it. For this reason, they should be kept on a soft sand substrate similar to the needs of Corydoradinae species. Another aspect they share with this subfamily is the way they spawn. Pitbull plecos spawn almost exactly like Corydoradinae species where the females will lay their eggs on a smooth surface after which males will fertilize said eggs.

  With all their similarities behaviorally to Corydoradinae, care needs and small size like an otocinclus, and their appearance resembling that of a traditional pleco, many describe the pitbull pleco to be like a combination of all three despite not belonging to any of these groups. Also like all three, they make absolutely fantastic additions to most planted or peaceful community tanks that match their required parameters. If all their needs are met, there is a chance you may even get these wonderful little Loricariids to spawn. We greatly encourage attempts at this as they are an excellent aquarium fish that we hope will now stay a staple of the hobby.

products.title: Black Ink Barb
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The rare black ink barb is seldom seen in the hobby despite being a very fascinating species with beautiful and unique coloration. Mature fish will display silver heads, yellow or orange tails, and a large, dark gray or inky black patch just below the dorsal fin. This unique pattern and the endearing set of whiskers on either side of their mouths make the black ink barb a very attractive looking fish. Like with all barbs, there is little visual difference between males and females, but usually if they are ready to spawn, males will display brighter colors and females will appear more rounded. 

  As not many have kept this species, not much is known about their care. What is known, is that like nearly all barbs, they are best kept in groups of 6-8 or more to satisfy their need to shoal and to disperse any aggression amongst competing males. Keeping black ink barbs in large groups will also help deter them from nipping other tank mates as they will be too busy establishing their own pecking order. Native to the Sebuku and Sesayap rivers in the North Kalimantan Province in Borneo, they can be found in both clear and blackwater areas with moderate current that provides plenty of dissolved oxygen and a sandy or rocky substrate. These areas are known to have fairly soft and moderate to acidic pH along with fallen branches and plenty of vegetation. For the black ink barb to thrive in captivity, it is best to mimic their natural habitat by keeping them in similar conditions. To satisfy their omnivorous diet, those keeping this species should provide a varied and high quality diet that consists of both meat and vegetable matter.

  If kept properly, the black ink barb can be a fantastic addition to those with larger South East Asian tanks or those looking for a larger, less common shoaling fish. They have potential to make great tankmates for the ever popular roseline shark as well. With their rarity, active behavior, and beautiful coloration, they can also warrant being kept in a species only tank. If this option is chosen, we encourage attempts at breeding this rare species as they are listed as vulnerable by the IUCN due to habitat destruction.

products.title: Avocado Puffer
products.body_html:
Remarks: The avocado puffer, also known as the green, bronze, golden, or metallic puffer, is a unique species of true freshwater puffer. Unlike most puffers that don't have a body-plan built for speed, avocado puffers are very stream-lined and are quite the fast swimmer compared to their relatives. This is due to them being adapted for the fast-flowing sections of rivers and streams in southeast Asia.

  Being so agile and like most other puffers, having the possibility of being very predatory and belligerent in regards to other fish, the avocado puffer is not an ideal choice for most community tanks. Since these intelligent fish have great variation in their personalities, occasionally you can find one that does just fine with faster swimming tank mates such as medium to large sized danios, more often than not the avocado puffer will harass and hunt down most fish they are housed with. To prevent losses, it is recommended that they be kept only with the fastest of swimmers or in a species only tank. Sometimes you will even need to keep them as a single, solitary fish as occasionally you can get one that is extremely aggressive even to its own kind. As they make up their natural diet, invertebrates of any kind are also not recommended to be housed with the avocado puffer unless they are intended as food for it.

  The avocado puffer at least is a fascinating enough fish to keep to warrant having a tank all to themselves. With how intelligent they are, it can be fascinating to observe their different personalities and how they investigate anything placed in their tank. Frequently they learn who their keeper is and will come right up to the glass to beg for food. It is good to provide lots of enrichment for these puffers to keep them entertained and to discourage destructive behavior. Not only are they entertaining to watch, avocado puffers also have very unique and attractive coloration. Their bellies tend to be white with their top halves being anywhere from bronze, to green, to even gold. These colors shine in the light as they are covered in a metallic sheen making for a true show-stopper of a puffer. 

  Like with all puffers, avocado puffers have a set of sharp teeth shaped like a beak that are constantly growing. These teeth need to be worn down otherwise they will grow to the point the puffer can no longer eat. To prevent this, the avocado needs to frequently be fed live foods with hard shells such as shrimp, snails, crab, or crayfish.

products.title: Albino Sailfin Pleco (Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps)
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The sailfin pleco (Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps) is one of the many species frequently misidentified as Hypostomus plecostomus, common pleco. They share a lot of similarities such as a similar body shape, large adult size, dark adult pattern, and nearly identical behaviors. The easiest way to identify that a fish is in fact a sailfin pleco is to look for their very tall sailfin which gives them their common name. Juvenile sailfin plecos also have a very attractive pattern with an almost rusty orange body and fins covered in large black spots that get smaller with age. While it is frequently misidentified, this species is a staple within the aquarium keeping hobby due to their high availability, voracious appetite for algae, and their hardy nature. 

  These traits along with their elegant sailfin make the sailfin pleco a highly desirable species that is commonly picked up by beginner keepers that are unaware of the large amounts of space an adult specimen will require. While most top out at around 19-20" which is already an impressive size, there are many reports of some reaching 24" or larger. This combined with their large appetite that can quickly result in fouled water and their high activity level make it where the sailfin pleco needs a very large tank to remain healthy, a minimum of 150 gallons being needed for just a single specimen. They tend to do best in warm water ponds as they allow for a lot more space to swim, grow, and feed. If keeping albino specimens, however, they should be kept indoors or in ponds with plenty of shade as they are sensitive to light. Unfortunately, it is common for sailfin plecos to outgrow their keeper's tanks which sometimes results in some getting released into the wild. This should never be done and any that outgrow a tank should be rehomed instead.

  For those that can provide the large amounts of space and heavy water changes required by the sailfin pleco, they can be an excellent choice to house alongside larger, more aggressive species such as bass, oscars, gar, and arowana. They are robust enough and an aggressive enough feeder to hold their own against these species. This does come with the trade-off that they do not make good tankmates for conspecifics as they frequently will harm them in territory fights or starve them out as they will almost always win in a fight over food.

products.title: Fire Ant Tetra
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The fire ant tetra is one of many new and exciting tetras that have been described in recent years. Having been described in 2017, fire ant tetras took the fish keeping hobby by storm with the regular males showing a vivid orange color and dominant males displaying some of the most vibrant red displayed by any tetra currently known. Unlike many other tetra species, they display very clear sexual dimorphism with the females having no red or orange colors and instead having greenish-silver bodies. Both sexes have a black, horizontal stripe that extends from the gill plate all the way into the center of the caudal fin with it being more apparent in females. This clear difference in appearance among mature males and females makes selecting fish for a breeding project much easier compared to other tetras.

  The common name for the fire ant tetra comes from more than just its fire red colors and scientific name, it also comes from the river they were discovered in. The Rio Formiga, known as the the "Ant River" in English, is the tributary in which this new species was discovered. Their natural habitat comprises of clean, clear water which is very odd as most red colored tetras that are this small usually inhabit black or red water areas where their coloration helps them blend in. As they come from water nearly completely devoid of pollutants, fire ant tetras will not tolerate poor water quality so frequent water changes and careful attention to water parameters are a must. While being a very small species, they also require a large amount of space to feel most comfortable and display their best colors as competition among males can get very fierce. Providing lots of structure such as with driftwood and live plants can further help reduce this aggression and boost their confidence making them hide less. Fire ant tetras make their need for space and careful attention to water parameters absolutely worth it, however, with their high amounts of activity and brilliant red color that makes them an excellent addition to many peaceful community tanks.

products.title: Blueberry OB Fryeri
products.body_html: Remarks:  Blueberry OB Fryeri are a hybrid man-made strain likely produced by crossing Sciaenochromis fryeri and OB peacocks then line-breeding the offspring. The result is a fish with absolutely striking colors and the elongated body shape of the Sciaenochromis fryeri. A majority of the males have platinum or light pink bodies covered in electric blue blotches. As blueberry OB Fryeri are hybrids, there tends to be lots of variation between each fish with some also displaying yellow and orange colors. Females and juveniles are usually devoid of color, being mostly gray or brown.

products.title: Neon Gold Rosy Barb
products.body_html: Remarks: Rosy barbs can tolerate a wide range of water parameters and are suitable for most community tank set-ups. Males darken their colors and turn deep red with yellow-green scales in the upper half of the body. Females are a lighter orange color and typically more rounded. They are peaceful barbs and tend to do best with some plant cover. The neon gold color variant presents much brighter, almost glowing colors than normal rosy barbs and have a sort of glitter effect on their bodies that makes them shimmer in the light.

products.title: Blue Face Heckel Discus "Nhamunda"
products.body_html:
Remarks:   Heckel discus are one of the most colorful, challenging to keep, and oldest known wild discus variants. They display vibrant yellows, oranges, greens, and blues that vary in amount and pattern depending on the exact locality they are sourced from.  Typically, Heckel discus are identified by the particularly wide and dark band that runs down the center of their body that is particularly round even for a discus. The Nhamunda blue face Heckel discus is a highly sought after locality as they display some of the most electric blue coloration found in any wild discus with most of it concentrated toward their heads. For some, this color is so rich it covers up the orange striping near the face. This combined with them being found in the Rio Nhamunda is where they get their common name. Care should be taken in regards to water parameters as these discus are wild and much less tolerant of incorrect water parameters. For the best chance at being successful, keep them in very soft, acidic, warm water and perform frequent water changes. 

  Discus are often referred to as the king of aquarium fish. This nickname arose from the sheer beauty of these fish along with their reputation for being difficult to care for making those who keep them successfully revered within the hobby. It being difficult to keep discus was an opinion developed when majority of specimens available were wild caught. With most now being captive bred and even line bred to produce many different color variants such as turquoise and pigeon blood, they are much more hardy. That being said, discus still require very clean, soft, acidic water with little to no nitrates and warm temperatures.

  These peaceful cichlids tend to be timid, especially when it comes to feeding, so they shouldn't be kept with overly boisterous or aggressive fish. Typically their mouths are very small, but once they reach adulthood they are capable of swallowing very tiny fish so this should also be considered when choosing tankmates. If attempting to breed discus, lots of vertical surfaces should be provided for them to lay their eggs on and it should be noted they can become territorial when spawning. Once the fry hatch and are free-swimming, both parents show an incredible form of parental care in which they feed their fry by allowing them to eat their slime coat. This behavior is an amazing sight and makes breeding discus very enjoyable.

products.title: Red Tail Penguin Tetra 'Rio Teles Pires'
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The red tail penguin tetra is a new take on a classic that has taken the aquarium scene by storm in recent years. Penguin tetras have been a staple in the hobby due to their hardiness, active swimming patterns, peaceful disposition, simple but attractive coloration, and ease of breeding. This locality shares all of these characteristics but with the addition of a bright red tail that pairs beautifully with their silver and black bodies and really makes them stand out.

  In 2017, the red tail penguin tetra was discovered in the Rio Teles Pires which is one of the headwaters of the Rio Tapajos that is known for its vast biodiversity. It was exported in fairly limited numbers and commanded a hefty price tag for wild caught individuals. However, with these fish now being bred in fairly large numbers in Germany and Indonesia, somewhat more affordable captive bred fish are available within the hobby. These fish are also more hardy compared to wild caught individuals, but still do best when kept in soft, acidic conditions.

  Like most smaller tetras, red tail penguin tetras should be kept in groups of 6 or more to make them feel secure and to reduce any nipping behavior toward other species. Providing lots of hiding spaces such as live plants will further boost their confidence and can help trigger breeding behavior along with brighter coloration.

products.title: Threadfin Cichlid (Petrochromis trewavasae)
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Threadfin cichlids (Petrochromis trewavasae) are a species of Petrochromis which are a larger sized group of Tanganyikan cichlids They are known for their rarity, beautiful coloration, unique mouths, and highly territorial behavior. This particular species has an elegant coloration and pattern that is very similar to a juvenile Tropheus duboisi. Their bodies and fins are a dark gray or black with white spots being dotted all over their bodies and faces. Dominant males will also show gold egg spots on their anal fins and gold accents along their belly, back, and face making for some very attractive contrast. For some, this gold color will actually spread even further resulting in a gold fish with white spots! Both black and gold males will also develop long trailers on the tips of their fins which is where their common name originates. Many note this species is not quite as aggressive as other Petrochromis so they can be a great introduction to the genus.

  Petrochromis are similar to Tropheus in that their natural diet is mostly comprised of algae and they are sensitive to protein, too much of which can cause bloat. Their large, protruding lips are indicative of this diet as they aid them in scraping rocks and other surfaces for algae. Another similarity they have with Tropheus is that they are best kept in a species only tank with at least 10-12 individuals in a ratio of about 5-6 females for every male. These large numbers are needed because while they feel the most comfortable and confident in groups, Petrochromis species are still extremely territorial and have a vicious pecking order. If there are not enough fish to spread out the aggression, the smallest or at least less dominant fish will be picked off until there is only one left. These cichlids also do not tolerate poor water quality or incorrect parameters. Therefore, to keep these fish in large numbers with lots of territory space and pristine water, the absolute minimum tank size would be a 150 gallon tank. 200 gallons or more is much more ideal, however. All these aspects make Petrochromis a fairly difficult species to keep long term but their fascinating behaviors, beautiful colors, and rarity make it worth it for the advanced keeper.

products.title: Black Eye Koi Guppy
products.body_html:
Remarks:  A perfect starter fish! Guppies are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. Black eye koi guppies display a form of partial albinism with their bodies lacking black pigment but still having black eyes instead of pink. They typically have white/platinum bodies and bright orange heads, tails, and fins. As these beautiful fish have pigment in their eyes, they are not as sensitive to light compared to complete albinos.

  For most strains, males are very colorful while the females remain a gray or silver color. However, there are some exceptions to this rule so sexing these fish is best done by looking at the anal fin. The females will have short, rounded triangle shaped anal fins with a black spot on the body directly above it, these are her female reproductive organs. Males will have long, thin, pointed anal fins that form his male reproductive organs called the gonopodium.

products.title: Japanese Blue Lyretail Guppy
products.body_html:
Remarks:  A perfect starter fish! Guppies are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. This line of guppies is both unique and beautiful with their elegant lyretails that shine with an electric blue color. This vibrant hue is complemented by the silver or tan front halves of their body that occasionally has spots that can be red, green, or black. It should be noted that the original lines likely were produced by hybridizing guppies and endlers so they are most likely not a pure species but this goes for most modern strains. 

  For most strains, males are very colorful while the females remain a gray or silver color. However, there are some exceptions to this rule so sexing these fish is best done by looking at the anal fin. The females will have short, rounded triangle shaped anal fins with a black spot on the body directly above it, these are her female reproductive organs. Males will have long, thin, pointed anal fins that form his male reproductive organs called the gonopodium.

products.title: Israeli Strain Japan Blue Gold Lyretail Guppy
products.body_html:
Remarks:  A perfect starter fish! Guppies are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. This line of guppies were originally produced in Israel which has gained a reputation for producing some of the most beautiful and healthy modern guppy lines. It should be noted that the original lines likely were produced by hybridizing guppies and endlers so they are most likely not a pure species but this goes for most modern strains. Now we breed them here on site to be able to offer these fish to fishkeepers in the United States. They stand out in just about any aquarium with their bodies being a bright gold and their lyretails a striking blue.

  For most strains, males are very colorful while the females remain a gray or silver color. However, there are some exceptions to this rule so sexing these fish is best done by looking at the anal fin. The females will have short, rounded triangle shaped anal fins with a black spot on the body directly above it, these are her female reproductive organs. Males will have long, thin, pointed anal fins that form his male reproductive organs called the gonopodium.

products.title: German Platinum Ribbon Fin Guppy
products.body_html:
Remarks:  A perfect starter fish! Guppies (Poecilia reticulata) are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females.

  These beautiful guppies were sourced from a German line that is now bred here at Imperial Tropicals. They have platinum white fins present on both males and some of the females. However, the males typically are the only ones to get the white or greenish-gold bodies. Through rigorous selective breeding, we have made this line even better with elegant, flowing ribbon fins that are present on both males and females! These are some truly gorgeous and unique guppies that are must have for any collector.

  For most strains, males are very colorful while the females remain a gray or silver color. However, there are some exceptions to this rule so sexing these fish is best done by looking at the anal fin. The females will have short, rounded triangle shaped anal fins with a black spot on the body directly above it, these are her female reproductive organs. Males will have long, thin, pointed anal fins that form his male reproductive organs called the gonopodium.

products.title: Snow White Platinum Guppy
products.body_html:
Remarks:  A perfect starter fish! Guppies are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. This line of guppies are absolutely breath-taking with their stark white bodies that seem to glow with how pure white the majority of their bodies and fins are resulting in an elegant look. 

  For most strains, males are very colorful while the females remain a gray or silver color. However, there are some exceptions to this rule so sexing these fish is best done by looking at the anal fin. The females will have short, rounded triangle shaped anal fins with a black spot on the body directly above it, these are her female reproductive organs. Males will have long, thin, pointed anal fins that form his male reproductive organs called the gonopodium.

products.title: Hyphessobrycon vinaceus
products.body_html:
Remarks: If you are looking for an extremely rare and almost never seen in the hobby species of tetra, Hyphessobrycon vinaceus is it. While it was described back in the early 2000s, it is brand new to the hobby. Because of this, it has yet to be given a common name and there is not much information in regards to their care. For species like this, it is best to replicate their natural habitat to have the best chance at success and maybe even get them to spawn.

  Hyphessobrycon vinaceus come from the Rio Pardo drainage in Brazil, specifically the Rio São João. The area of collection was specifically in a portion of rapids directly below a waterfall with a sandy bottom. From this, it can be concluded this species requires, clear, highly oxygenated water with a good amount of current. When healthy and mature, this species displays a body that varies from silver, to gold, to green and a black, horizontal bar along the lateral line that starts at the base of the caudal fin and fades up into the middle of the body. When mature and in the right conditions, below that bar and on the back half of the fish is a vibrant red patch of color and just behind the eye on the gill plate is a black spot preceded by a spot of green iridescence. Between this beautiful coloration and its rarity, this fish is a must have for tetra collectors.

products.title: Scarlet Badis
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Scarlet badis are a gem of a nano fish with their bright red bodies and fins accented by electric blue stripes and edges. The amount of color packed into such a tiny fish is truly impressive. Males use this bright color to court females that are notably duller, usually being mostly tan. As territorial battles amongst these tiny fish can get very violent, sometimes less dominant males will pretend to be females by also displaying muted colors so they are less likely to be attacked. 

  In the wild, these fish can be found in shallow, blackwater pools and streams in India. The water in these pools is very warm, mildly acidic, soft, and full of botanicals such as fallen leaves and seed pods. These botanicals provide a habitat that supports a wide variety of tiny worms and crustaceans that scarlet badis feed on. While tiny, these little fish are voracious in their hunting of these small organisms so they are often referred to as micro predators. This makes them a poor choice to keep alongside cherry shrimp as they will hunt down baby shrimplets and if hungry and bold enough, occasionally take down full grown shrimp! Since they can be rather picky and have very fast metabolisms, scarlet badis should be fed small, live foods such as moina or baby brine shrimp at least daily if not multiple times a day.

  Care should also be taken when choosing tankmates as most fish will either attempt to eat the scarlet badis or will frighten it as they are very timid. Most do best when kept as pairs in a species-only tank. One can be kept in a 5 gallon, while a pair or more will require a 10 gallon or larger. Due to their need for live foods and cryptic nature, they are not a good beginner fish. However, for the more experienced keeper, they are well worth the effort once they begin to show their intense colors and inquisitive personalities.

products.title: Dalmatian Molly
products.body_html:
Remarks: A perfect starter fish! Mollies are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. Fish with long fins such as guppies should be avoided as mollies tend to be fin-nippers.

  This line of mollies are absolutely beautiful and unique with their platinum white bodies and black spots similar to the dalmatian breed of dog. They stand out in just about any tank, but especially against a backdrop of green plants and driftwood. Using a dark substrate as well will really make their elegant color pop!

products.title: Zebra Gold Mbuna
products.body_html: Remarks: The zebra gold mbuna is a brightly colored, medium-sized mbuna. Males are a golden yellow with black ventral fins and light blue details on their other fins. Their face and belly are black to blue. Females are gray to silver and occasionally have a few light blue markings. These fish are mainly herbivorous, feeding on algae and plankton in the wild. Zebra gold mbunas are aggressive toward similar looking species but are generally non-combative toward non-conspecifics making them a good species to keep alongside blue colored mbuna.

products.title: Zebra Gold "Ruarwe"
products.body_html:
Remarks: This locality of zebra gold mbunas are found on the northwestern side of Lake Malawi in the deeper, rocky areas near Ruarwe. Males are a golden yellow with black ventral fins and light blue details on their other fins. Their face and belly are black to blue. Females are gray to silver and occasionally have a few light blue markings. These fish are mainly herbivorous, feeding on algae and plankton in the wild. Zebra gold mbunas are aggressive toward similar looking species but are generally non-combative toward non-conspecifics.

Scientific Name: Metriaclima sp. "Ruarwe"

Common Name: Zebra Gold "Ruarwe"

Max Size: 6 inches

pH: 7.5-8.4

Hardness: Hard

Temperature: 74-82°

Aggressiveness: Aggressive

Region of Origin: Lake Malawi, Africa

Captive Bred or Wild: Captive Bred

Diet: Flake or Pellet food low in protein

Compatibility: Mbuna

Tank Mate Options:

  • Williamsi Blue Lips
  • White Tail Acei
  • Pseudotropheus socolofi
*****Please review our Shipping and Handling FAQ prior to placing your order. It contains important information about order fulfillment time, shipping speed, and other pertinent details.*****

products.title: Crystal Red Tetra
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The crystal red tetra is a beautiful gem of a nano fish native to the Rio Araguaia in Brazil. In the wild, their intensely bright red coloration helps them blend into the reddish-brown water of tannin-stained tributaries. Mature, healthy specimens in captivity display this brilliant red color more than any other tetra when in the hobby which makes them look absolutely incredible when kept among green plants. Most will note this species bears a striking resemblance to young specimens of the serpae tetra, but they can be told apart by the fact the crystal red tetra has a smaller black spot on the dorsal fin and that it has white tips on its fins. As it was only described in the past few years, is rarely imported, and is very difficult to spawn for a tetra, they are fairly rare within the hobby.

  Housing the crystal red tetra in a heavily planted tank will make these shy fish feel brave enough to come out more readily. Their shy nature and small size does mean care should be taken when choosing tankmates, ensuring the other species they are housed with are not aggressive or large enough to swallow them. If provided the right conditions, these little fish add plenty of movement to the tank. They will dart around exploring every corner of the tank and performing display dances as males compete for females. If you are looking to add a splash of red to your tank, these rare little tetras can be an excellent choice.

  The scientific name for the crystal red tetra used to be Hyphessobrycon haraldschultzi. However, it was reclassified as Megalamphodus haraldschultzi after the completion of an intensive phylogenic study on species in the family Characidae. Many other species were reclassified as well as a result of this study which can be read here: https://academic.oup.com/zoolinnean/article-abstract/202/1/zlae101/7748269

  The following article also provides a good breakdown of the results of the study and does not have a paywall: https://aquainfo.org/the-end-of-the-characidae-family-as-we-know-it/

products.title: L031 Peppermint Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The L031 peppermint pleco is an elegant loricariid from the Rio Xingu in Brazil. They live in areas with strong currents and rocky bottoms where they hide in the cracks between rocks. Most would suspect that conditions like this would result in the L031 peppermint pleco preferring cooler water, but quite the opposite is true as they prefer warmer temperatures compared to other loricariids. They do, however, still require very pristine water with a high oxygen content so heavy aeration and frequent water changes are a must.

  If cared for properly, this medium sized pleco can be a dazzling addition to the aquarium. Their dark brown to jet black bodies and fins look like the night sky with the white speckles that cover them. Typically these spots start out larger while the fish is younger and decrease in size but increase in abundance as they age. While they will eat algae, L031 peppermint plecos are omnivores and require a varied diet of both vegetarian and carnivorous foods to remain healthy. Providing plenty of hiding spaces will make this species feel safe enough to venture out more for you to see their beautiful patterns and fascinating behavior.

products.title: Geophagus Altifrons
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Geophagus altifrons is a very uncommon, yet highly sought after species of eartheater. This is due in part to their fantastic coloration with healthy adult specimens displaying orange/red fins dotted with blue and bodies covered in electric blue scales. These scales practically glow especially in tannin-stained water. Both males and females can grow impressively long trailers especially on their ventral fins, but they do tend to get a bit longer on males.

  The native range of Geophagus altifrons spans across many tributaries in the middle to lower Amazon basin and crosses over with many other species of Geophagus. This crossing of native ranges along with how similar they are to other species has resulted in many fish being misidentified as Geophagus altifrons. It is extremely difficult if not impossible to tell them apart from some other species when they are juveniles, but adults do show some defining features. When looking to confirm if a fish is truly a Geophagus altifrons, some features to look for are as follows: an absence of vertical stress bars no matter the condition of the fish, lack of a dark spot on the gill plate, and the most defining feature of either a very small dark spot on the middle of the body only two scales in width or a complete absence of the spot entirely.

  Like all Geophagus, Geophagus altifrons require a sandy substrate due to their earth-eating habits. They sift mouthfuls of sand through their gills, pulling out bits of food to eat. Gravel presents a very high probability of choking or refusing to eat. Also like their close relatives, these fish do best in groups of 4 or more but can also do well in single male and female pairs. Due to their large size, adult Geophagus altifrons should be kept in an aquarium that is at least 90 gallons if kept as a single, breeding pair, but 125 gallons or more is needed if they are to be kept with other fish. When spawning, they can become territorial as they are protective parents and they have a good chance of swallowing tank mates that can fit in their mouths. These behaviors should be taken into consideration when planning a tank for these wonderful fish.

products.title: Tuira's Whiptail
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Tuira's whiptail is an uncommon species of whiptail not often imported into the United States. In the wild, they are actually not found in the Rio Tuira, but rather in the fast flowing section of the Rio Xingu where they get their other common name, the marbled Xingu whiptail. The Tuira part of their common name actually comes from the fish being named after a Kayapo woman that was considered a symbol of résistance in regards to hydroelectric dams being built on the Rio Xingu. Another fascinating thing to note about this species is that it ended a 70 year long time-frame in which no new species in the Spatuloricaria had been described once it was first recorded in 2014.

  As they are a rapids-dwelling species, Tuira's whiptails require pristine water and high levels of dissolved oxygen to thrive. They are a smaller whiptail with the males actually being the smallest of their genus, typically maxing out at 5-6" in length. Another unique aspect is that they are the only members of their genus where the females grow larger than the males, typically growing to 6-7" in length. This makes them a good choice for medium sized aquariums as they do not require quite as much spaces as other, larger whiptail species. The Tuira's whiptail can be a great choice if you are looking for an omnivorous whiptail that won't be too much competition for species like Otocinclus catfish as they tend to not care much for algae. Instead, they should be provided a varied diet consisting of both meaty and vegetable based foods. If all their needs are met, these whiptails can be fascinating talking point for your South American community tank.

products.title: Xingu Black Neon Tetra
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Xingu black neon tetra is new to the hobby and rarely imported. As their common name suggests, they are visually similar to the more common black neon tetra, Hyphessobrycon herbertaxelrodi, but they have a lot of differences. Unlike the regular black neon tetra that can be found in calmer waters in the more southern regions of South America, the Xingu black neon are found in the extreme rapids of the more northern Rio Xingu in Brazil. This species also remains a bit smaller and shows more color. Rather than just displaying the typical black and tan color with a blue stripe along the lateral line, they also have bright yellow belly when healthy. The contrast between the yellow and the blue looks beautiful and unique in the aquarium especially among green plants.

  As these rare tetras come from areas of high flow, it is important that they are provided plenty of aeration in the aquarium to remain healthy. Soft, clean, acidic water is also necessary. Like most tetras, Xingu black neon tetras are highly social and will become stressed if not maintained in a group of at least 6 fish with 10 or more being ideal. The larger the school, the more secure they will feel making them more likely to venture out where you can view them actively darting about the tank.

products.title: L137 Blue Eyed Red Fin Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks: The L137 blue eyed red fin pleco is a beautiful and uncommon species of Hypostomus from Brazil. Like others in the same genus, they have high backs, tall dorsal fins, and spoon-shaped teeth that aid them in grazing on algae and plant matter. The color of this species is variable with their bodies ranging from a soft gray to a warm brown, accented by their fins that can be anywhere from a dark brownish-red to a burnt orange. Sometimes these fins even have spots. These fins along with their striking blue eyes give them their common name. The second half of their scientific name, soniae, was given in honor of loricariid expert, Sonia Fisch-Muller who took part in its discovery.

  In the aquarium, the L137 blue eyed red fin pleco can be a great choice for an algae-eater to live alongside large, South American cichlids. They have a voracious appetite and can make quick work of many different species of algae but still need to have their diets supplemented with wood, fresh vegetables, and high-quality pellets. This appetite coincides with a high metabolism so frequent water changes are necessary to manage their high waste output. These plecos can be rather territorial and boisterous so it is best not to house them with other loricariids unless they are in a very large system. This behavior, however makes them very good at handling cichlids with similar demeanors so they pair well with cichlids such as Salvin's cichlids and large severums.

products.title: L114 Demini Leopard Cactus Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The L114 Demini leopard cactus pleco is a massive and beautiful species of Loricariid popular among those with tanks based around monster South American fish. Their common name lends credence to their yellow or tan bodies covered in black leopard spots as well as the sharp armor covering their bodies making them very prickly. This armor is made up of pointed structures called odontodes that help the pleco wedge itself in hiding spaces as well as discourage predators from attempting to eat it. Adding to their unique look, the L114 Demini leopard cactus pleco has a brilliant caudal fin and edge to their dorsal fin showing coloration that ranges from a vibrant gold to a rich orange.

  There are actually a few different color variations and localities of this species, each being referred to by a specific L number. This particular locality is known from the Rio Demini and is identified by how the spots on their heads are about the same size as the spots on their bodies. Within their natural range, they inhabit acidic, soft, warm, blackwater areas filled with driftwood. To have the most success with keeping them in captivity, these conditions should be replicated while also providing plenty of aeration as is needed by all Loricariids.

  As beautiful as these catfish are, they are not the easiest to care for. L114 Demini leopard cactus plecos can be very aggressive with other loricariids especially if not provided ample territory space and caves. It also can be a challenge to keep up with their needs in regards to parameters as while they produce a lot of waste, they do not tolerate pollutants such as high nitrate levels. Consequentially, they require large, frequent water changes to maintain the pristine conditions they need. Their diet also must be taken into account as they are omnivorous and need a varied diet to remain healthy. Repashy products can be a great option for feeding them. While typically docile toward non-conspecifics, L114 Demini leopard cactus plecos have been reported eating fish that fit in their mouths so care should be taken when choosing tank mates. Larger characins such as chalceus and headstanders can be good options for tank mates that can't be swallowed and won't take away from the beauty of this amazing catfish.

products.title: L018 Gold Nugget Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The L018 gold nugget pleco is a highly coveted species of Loricariid within the aquarium keeping hobby and one look at them shows exactly why this is. With their dark gray to black bodies decorated with contrasting bright gold/yellow seams along the edges of their dorsal and caudal fins along with similarly colored spots, they are considered one of the most beautiful plecos. These seams and spots are thicker and larger when the fish is younger, reducing in size as they grow but gaining even more of these spots. This changes their pattern from a playful polka-dot one to a more elegant speckle or starry pattern. While many would presume their common name to be derived from their gold/yellow pattern, it actually has a much more interesting story.

  When the gold mining industry in the native range, the Rio Xingu, of the L018 gold nugget pleco shut down, locals turned to these abundant plecos as a source of income. Young fish are collected for the aquarium trade while older fish are collected for food. Capturing these fish is far from an easy endeavor, however, as they live in some of the fastest flowing water in the Amazon and hide among the cracks in boulders where they graze on biofilm and algae as well as capture small insect larvae and crustaceans. These conditions consisting of clear, pristine, warm, oxygen-rich waters full of hiding spots should be replicated in the aquarium if one wants to keep L018 gold nugget plecos. If these conditions are not met, this sensitive species will almost certainly decline in health over time and eventually die.

  As this omnivorous catfish mainly feeds on algae and vegetable matter, this should consist of a majority of their diet in captivity. However, they are still omnivores so protein still should be provided. Due to L018 gold nugget plecos actually having softer teeth compared to other species, they prefer softer foods so Repashy gel foods are a great choice for feeding them. It should also be noted their metabolism is rather fast for how long their intestinal tract is so they will need to be fed more frequently than most other Loricariids.

  Keeping multiple L018 gold nugget plecos can be challenging as they are a very territorial species, aggressively sparring to the point of injury and sometimes even death if ample space is not provided. A 60 gallon tank can be enough for a single male and a small group of females, but a minimum of 100 gallons is needed if one plans to keep more than one male. This aggression can also be shown to conspecifics so they are best kept as the only pleco in the tank or even in a species only tank. Needless to say, this is not a beginner species of pleco and is more suited for the intermediate to advanced keeper. If well cared for, it is well worth the challenge to keep such a beautiful and unique species.
