products.title: Kohaku Koi Swordtail
products.body_html:
Remarks: Kohaku koi swordtails possess a patch of reddish-orange on their head and on their tail with a silvery-white body in-between. "Kohaku" means "amber" in Japanese which is fitting for such an intensely colored fish. Their vibrant reddish-orange patches and high contrast results in a striking fish that stands out well in just about any aquarium.

  Swordtails are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. Dominant males will begin developing swords and a modified anal fin, called a gonopodium, around 1-1.5" in length. However, it is not uncommon for less dominant males to maintain the physical appearance of a female until much later in life (even as large as 4"!) Some have also reported the strange phenomenon of these fish occasionally changing gender with it typically occurring with older, large females becoming male with mixed results in regards to them remaining fertile.

products.title: Anubias congensis
products.body_html:
Remarks: Anubias congensis is perfect for any planted aquarium. It is relatively undemanding and is a low light plant native to Africa. What makes this anubias unique is that its leaves are fairly long and pointed compared to more common anubias such as nana. Their leaves also tend to reach up toward the surface of the tank rather than lay flat.

  Anubias spp. should be tied to a rock, driftwood, or other decor, not planted into the substrate. If the rhizome, the thick main stem, is buried, it will likely rot away from being smothered. It will eventually grab and propagate itself to whatever it's tied to or wedged into using its strong roots.

You will receive a medium sized plant with 4-6 leaves.

If you are only ordering plants, you are eligible for a shipping rate of $19.99. Please include a note or email requesting a partial shipping refund when you order.

*Plants may contain snails and/or snail eggs.

***** Please read our SHIPPING AND HANDLING FAQ before ordering. It contains important information about order fulfillment time, shipping speed, etc. *****

products.title: Anubias afzelii
products.body_html:
Remarks: Anubias afzelii is perfect for any planted aquarium. It is relatively undemanding and is a low light plant native to Africa. What makes this anubias unique is that its leaves are fairly long and pointed compared to more common anubias such as nana. Their leaves also tend to reach up toward the surface of the tank rather than lay flat. As they are very similar, this anubias is commonly misidentified as Anubias congensis. The difference is that Anubias afzelii grows larger and has some-what more oblong leaves.

  Anubias spp. should be tied to a rock, driftwood, or other decor, not planted into the substrate. If the rhizome, the thick main stem, is buried, it will likely rot away from being smothered. It will eventually grab and propagate itself to whatever it's tied to or wedged into using its strong roots.

You will receive a medium sized plant with 4-6 leaves.

If you are only ordering plants, you are eligible for a shipping rate of $19.99. Please include a note or email requesting a partial shipping refund when you order.

*Plants may contain snails and/or snail eggs.

***** Please read our SHIPPING AND HANDLING FAQ before ordering. It contains important information about order fulfillment time, shipping speed, etc. *****

products.title: Red Kachese Tropheus
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The red Kachese tropheus is an uncommon locality of Tropheus that can be found near Cape Kachese along the southern coast of Lake Tanganyika. Upon maturity and when in the right conditions, these fish have beautiful coloration. The top half of their bodies and their faces are dark gray or black making their golden bellies, reddish-orange fins occasionally flecked with blue, and blue lips really pop. These colors are more blended together and dull in juveniles but the wait for them to grow into their true potential is worth it as it is with all Tropheus.

  Tropheus are obligate herbivores, and even small amounts of protein can induce bloating conditions. They form social hierarchies and should be kept in large groups, about 10 or more, to spread out any aggression. Tropheus tend to be more peaceful toward non-conspecific species so they can do well in a large Tanganyikan community tank.

products.title: Xenotilapia sp. "Kilesa"
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Xenotilapia sp. "Kilesa" are a very rare species of Tanganyikan cichlid found on the western side of the lake. They aren't a very large species, with a long face used for sifting sand and a cigar-shaped body, they patrol the sandy bottom. While they are omnivores, they lean more toward being carnivores so they feed mostly on meaty foods such as tiny crustaceans and worms. Juveniles and females are silver in color while mature males show much more fantastic coloration. If in the right conditions, males display metallic blue scales on their bodies, an iridescent green sheen on their faces, yellow lips, black pectoral and anal fins, and a blue dorsal fin dotted with yellow spots. This coloration is absolutely beautiful and pairs well with the fascinating displays males put on to get the attention of females.

  In the aquarium, Xenotilapia sp. "Kilesa" are not the easiest Tanganyikan to care for. They do not tolerate poor water quality at all, are very skittish, and require a lot more space than one would expect such a small species would require. This is because males command large territories as they build multiple spawning pits that can span 2 feet in diameter. A tank with a footprint of at least 48" by 18," equivalent to that of a standard 75 gallon, should be considered the minimum. Xenotilapia sp. "Kilesa" also do best when kept in groups of 6 or more, preferably in a ratio of 2 males to 4 females. While males can be pretty feisty, their fights rarely become physical and they usually ignore other species that do not dwell on the bottom like they do. This makes them excellent tank mates for Cyprichromis species, though their interesting behaviors and eye-catching colors also make them a great choice for a species only tank.

  Due to the sensitive nature of these fish, DOA claims will only be honored if the fish are in their sealed bags. No exceptions will be made for losses that occur after opening the bags.

products.title: Fairy Moss
products.body_html:
Remarks: Fairy moss, also known as mosquito fern, is a fascinating species of floating plant native to the Western United States where it can be found in areas of stagnant water. It grows in a manner similar to duckweed where smaller parts of the plant will break off as it grows to become a new plant. Fairy moss is typically a vibrant green, but certain conditions such as high quality light and low nitrate levels can result in it turning a beautiful pink or red color. To remain healthy within the aquarium, this plant shouldn't be kept in areas of high flow and should be provided with medium to high light. As long as these conditions are met, this small, floating plant can be a great addition to both small and large aquariums or ponds.

  Photos show one portion which is approximately 2 oz. 

If you are only ordering plants, you are eligible for a shipping rate of $19.99. Please include a note or email requesting a partial shipping refund when you order.

*Plants may contain snails and/or snail eggs and duckweed.

products.title: WYSIWYG Starry Night Cichlid Male
products.body_html:
You will receive the exact fish in the photos/video. 

Remarks: The starry night cichlid is possibly the most popular of the cichlids native to Madagascar within the hobby. They are known for their rich, black coloration and bright white speckles that looks like the night sky which explains where their common name originates from. This striking coloration is best brought out when this species is kept on a dark substrate under dim lighting. While these fish can be rather shy for a larger cichlid, they still can hold their against most other cichlids. They usually are very peaceful except when spawning during which the pair will claim a rather large territory space. Being piscivorous, despite their typically docile temperament, eat any fish small enough to fit in their mouths.

  Starry night cichlids are extremely adaptable in the wild with many different populations inhabiting a wide variety of habitats from cold, high altitude waters, to hot springs, to acidic blackwater pools, to even brackish estuaries. That being said, in captivity they do best in water parameters that are fairly neutral all around. Despite their broad range, starry night cichlids are still considered critically endangered in the wild due to habitat destruction and introduction of invasive species.

  There is some debate whether Paratilapia polleni and "P. bleekeri" represent two species or one, based primarily on the size and frequency of the spots.  It is said Bleekeri tend to be larger and have larger spots than Polleni. The most recent literature suggests that they both belong to P. polleni, but we have kept the "Bleekeri" name for those who collect the various types.

products.title: Parnaibae Sailfin Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Parnaibae sailfin pleco is possibly one of the most attractively patterned sailfin plecos known to the hobby. Their dark brown or black bodies and fins are covered in a mottled pattern of gold or tan striations and spots. Furthermore, they have endearing little "horns" on their nose and a very tall dorsal fin as is typical of their genus. This pleco is a fairly large species, sometimes reaching 15 inches in length, but most in captivity top out at around 12".

  In the wild, the Parnaibae sailfin pleco is only found in the Rio Parnaiba alongside the numerous other endemic species found there. Being omnivores, they will feed on anything from wood to fallen fruits, to vegetation, to insect larvae. They have proven to have quite the appetite like all sailfin plecos which results in lots of waste so frequent water changes are a must. Young specimens have a voracious appetite for algae so they can be great for cleanup in a smaller community tank until they grow large enough to go with bigger fish such as large, South American cichlids. With their thick armor and large size, they can protect themselves from these bigger, more boisterous fish.

products.title: Cryptocoryne wendtii "Tropica"
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Cryptocoryne wendtii "Tropica" is a variant of the ever popular Cryptocoryne wendtii. There is great variation in the colors that this plant can display, sometimes being mostly green, sometimes red, and even in rare cases, pink like the flamingo variant. Some do note that it tends to display a more obvious pattern of stripes or spots on its leaves compared to other variants. A lot of how this plant will appear depends on the conditions it is kept in, especially in regards to quality of light and substrate. 

  Cryptocoryne wendtii is a beautiful low to medium light plant. This is a great mid-ground plant which should do well in any planted aquarium. It is relatively undemanding if left alone and will grow into a nice clump and a max size of around 8".

If you are only ordering plants, you are eligible for a shipping rate of $19.99. Please include a note or email requesting a partial shipping refund when you order.

*Plants may contain snails and/or snail eggs.

***** Please read our SHIPPING AND HANDLING FAQ before ordering. It contains important information about order fulfillment time, shipping speed, etc. *****

products.title: Painted Swordtail
products.body_html:
Remarks: Painted swordtails are beautiful variety of swordtail that sport a red or orange body with variable black spots and patches that make it look like it were splattered with black paint. This black color tends to be more concentrated toward the back end of the fish but sometimes you can get an individual that has a more scattered pattern placement. The result is that no two fish look the same adding a unique touch to an already unique fish.

  Swordtails are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. Dominant males will begin developing swords and a modified anal fin, called a gonopodium, around 1-1.5" in length. However, it is not uncommon for less dominant males to maintain the physical appearance of a female until much later in life (even as large as 4"!) Some have also reported the strange phenomenon of these fish occasionally changing gender with it typically occurring with older, large females becoming male with mixed results in regards to them remaining fertile.

products.title: Colombian Green Severum
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Colombian green severums we currently have are wild caught and will be much less tolerant of pollutants and incorrect water parameters compared to our captive bred fish. However, this also means they will be pure compared to the likely hybrids that are common within the hobby and have much healthier genetics. 

  Severums have been a mainstay in the aquarium hobby for decades. They grow large enough to act as centerpieces, but they are not tank-busters, and they come in a variety of color patterns. These mid-sized cichlids are typically peaceful unless they are spawning. Small tank mates that can fit in their mouths and plants usually do not mix well with severums as they are likely to be eaten. Colombian green severums have a very elaborate body color, ranging from green to blue to red depending on light and mood. Sometimes they will even display a series of black vertical bars the run halfway up their body except for the one at the base of the tail that extends all the way to their back. A constant coloration among healthy specimens is their vibrant reddish-orange ventral and anal fins that provide a healthy flash of color.

variants.title: 1-2" Unsexed F1 Imperial Tropicals Captive Bred
variants.option1: 1-2" Unsexed F1 Imperial Tropicals Captive Bred

values: 1-2" Unsexed F1 Imperial Tropicals Captive Bred

products.title: Blue Blushing Veil-Tail Angelfish
products.body_html:
Remarks: Blue blushing veil-tail angelfish are a very uncommon color variant. They have white or silver bodies with dark colored fins similar to a Bulgarian green, but the color sheen they get when in the right light is blue instead of green. These fish also have the veil-tail gene which results in elegant, elongated fins. These longer fins are beautiful but can make these angels more of a target for fin-nippers so extra care should be taken when choosing tank mates.

  Angelfish are easy-going cichlids that do well in almost any community tank. Captive bred specimens can tolerate a wide range of pH values and are typically peaceful enough to be kept with most tetras and other peaceful South American cichlids. Due to their long ventral fins, we do not recommend keeping them with very nippy fish such as tiger barbs or zebra danios.

  Breeding Angelfish can often be achieved by giving them a vertical, flat surface to spawn on, such as a leaf of an Amazon Sword or a piece of slate leaning against the wall. Fry will be cared for until a few weeks after they are free swimming, though you can remove the fry or eggs and hatch them artificially if you wish. It is usually best to isolate breeding pairs in their own tank when spawning as they can become territorial.

products.title: Atromaculatus Cichlid
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Atromaculatus cichlid is an uncommon South American cichlid native to coastal drainages in Columbia. Unlike other fish within their genus such as festaes, they are typically fairly docile for a cichlid as long as their territory is not intruded on and the fish in question isn't small enough to be swallowed. Usually they will alter the space they consider their territory, digging in the sand and rearranging objects to their liking. The Atromaculatus cichlid is best kept in male-female pairs as males tend to fight if kept in the same space. Once a pair has formed, they will spawn on a flat surface with the female doing most of the guarding of eggs and fry. With how uncommon they are within the hobby, we encourage anyone getting these fish to attempt to breed them.

  Upon maturity and if in the right conditions, these fish get bright yellow coloration on their gill plates and sometimes along their backs. Their fins also gain a yellow or orange color, some even getting an iridescent blue spot in the dorsal fin as well, that looks nice against their silver bodies and black markings. Dominant males when in breeding dress will display a nice red belly with that coloration rising up toward the lateral line. With such coloration and a somewhat more docile personality, If given enough territory, Atromaculatus cichlids can be excellent additions to a large, South American cichlid tank or in a species only tank.

products.title: Spotted Highfin Hoplisoma
products.body_html:
Remarks: The spotted highfin Hoplisoma is native to the Rio Orinoco and Rio Meta. They are very similar in appearance to Hoplisoma melanistium and Brochis delphax with all three displaying a tan or gray body, a dark blotch on the "shoulder" that extends into the anterior half of the dorsal fin, a peppering of black speckles throughout the body, and a black "mask" across their eyes. What sets the spotted highfin Hoplisoma apart are the dark, broken vertical stripes in the caudal fin and its fairly short and round snout which is where its species name, brevirostris, comes from as it roughly translates to "short snout." 

  Spotted highfin Hoplisoma were previously classified as Corydoras brevirostris, but were reclassified as Hoplisoma brevirostris upon publication of a phylogenomic analysis in 2024 that reclassified many species in the Corydoradinae subfamily.

  Corydoradinae are small, peaceful catfish that are active shoalers and will stay near conspecifics, making for a lovely sight. They feed by searching through sand with their barbels, making such a substrate necessary for their long-term health. Gravel substrates tend to be abrasive and damage the barbels, leading to infections and an inability to find food.

  While these friendly catfish are willing to swim with other species of Corydoradinae different from their own, this does not provide the same social need as being kept with members of their own species. Because of this, it is highly recommended to keep at least six of each species of Corydoradinae present in the aquarium and only mix species if one has the space to keep at least six of each.

  Corydoradinae are often looked at as "scavengers," but they should be treated in a similar fashion as the rest of your fish. They are omnivores and require a mixed diet. Sinking prepared foods are readily accepted, as are frozen and live foods such as brine shrimp, blackworms, and bloodworms.

products.title: Jewel Spotted Doradid
products.body_html:
Remarks: The jewel spotted doradid, also known as the snoring catfish, is a large, rare catfish endemic to the Rio Jequitinhonha and Rio Pardo in Brazil. This rarity can be owed in part to the fact that these waters remain mostly untouched by the ornamental trade. Only once Brazilian exporter Hudson Crizanto Gonçalves began breeding these fish did they start to really appear in the trade. An interesting thing to note about them as well is that they are the only ones in their genus, differing from others in the Doradidae family in that it lacks retrorse thorns on their midlateral scutes.

  While the jewel spotted doradid doesn't have the most striking appearance as a juvenile, typically being a muted brownish-gray with a few white speckles, the adults really stand out especially against a black background. Upon maturity, these fish develop striking white or gold spots across their bodies which makes the wait absolutely worth it. While they also grow quite large, they are mostly peaceful toward non-conspecifics and anything that does not fit in their huge mouth. The jewel spotted doradid can be shy so keeping light low and providing plenty of hiding spots will encourage them to venture out more.

  With how rare this species is and their fantastic pattern, it is a must have for oddball catfish collectors

products.title: Chalinochromis sp. "Bifrenatus"
products.body_html:
Remarks: Chalinochromis sp. "Bifrenatus" is a mid-sized Tanganyikan cichlid found hiding in rock piles and away from the sandy areas of the lake. Like other Chalinochromis sp., they have bridle-like markings on the face (chalina in Greek translates to bridle).  What sets them apart from other species is that the markings continue down the length of their body in the form of two horizontal stripes along their lateral line rather than a series of dots.

  When in breeding dress, males will sometimes display striking blue coloration on the edges of their fins and a yellow body in order to catch the female's attention. Chalinochromis sp. "Bifrenatus" can be spawned by keeping a small group of fish until they pair off. Once a pair is formed, they will defend a spawning site, such as a flower pot or cave, and lay 40-60 eggs.

products.title: Blue Black-Banded Tetra
products.body_html:
Remarks: The blue black-banded tetra is fairly new to the hobby, having been described back in 2006 when it was discovered in the Rio Doze de Outubro which is a part of the Rio Tapajos basin. Here, it darts in the clear waters with fairly swift currents to fight against. They are very similar in appearance to the blue ribbon tetra, Hyphessobrycon sp., with both species displaying bright blue coloration on the top half of their bodies above a thick, black, horizontal band that extends from the back of the eye to the middle of the caudal fin. What sets the blue black-banded tetra apart is its more stream-lined appearance and the striking red coloration mostly present in courting or sparring males. This mix of blue and red looks excellent against a backdrop of green plants.

  This species is rather territorial with males constantly vying for dominance, and is very active. To have the most success, their habitat of fast-flowing, clear, highly oxygenated, streams should be replicated with plenty of horizontal space to swim and set up territories. These conditions are also usually preferred by most small, South American catfish, so they would be good options for tank mates. Blue black-banded tetra show behavior similar to that of barbs with them being very nippy even in regards to larger tankmates if they are not provided enough space and are not kept in large groups. Keep these fish in groups of 10 or more and tank mates with long fins should be avoided. If provided with these conditions, one will be rewarded with a truly stunning, rare fish and a more peaceful attitude.

products.title: Slate Pleco
products.body_html:
Remarks: The slate pleco is an uncommon, but amazing species of small Loricariid native to the Rio Jaguaribe. They have dark gray or black bodies mottled with white spots similar to that of L201 making for a nice contrast. As their genus, Pseudancistrus, entails, their body shape is similar to that of bushynose, Ancistrus, plecos in that they are rather short and stout. Unlike bushynose, their bristles are present on both males and females and are fairly short. Males and females are instead differentiated by head shape with the female's being shorter and more broad compared to that of a male. 

  With a fairly brave and curious nature, the behavior of the slate pleco makes it all the better for aquarium life. If kept in the right conditions, you can find this species exploring every inch of the aquarium. They can prove to be a rather helpful addition to a peaceful South American community in that they are mainly herbivorous, happily feeding on any surface algae they can get to. Furthermore, unlike most Loricariids found in the hobby, they prefer warmer water and don't get very large so they pair nicely with species such as German blue rams or cardinal tetras. It should be mentioned, however, that they do not tolerate poor water quality so frequent water changes are a must to keep this fascinating pleco happy and healthy.

products.title: Sertanejo Pleco
products.body_html: Remarks: The Sertanejo pleco is a slightly smaller relative of the common pleco that comes from the Rio Umbuzeiro in Brazil. Its habitat consists of clear, fast flowing, highly oxygenated streams and rapids containing soft sand and rounded stones. As they were only described back in 2017, they are still fairly new to the hobby. Like their common cousins, this pleco is rather hardy and accommodating of a fairly broad range of water parameters compared to most Loricariidae. What makes it even better, however, is its much more attractive pattern. When young, this fish is black with many large, yellow spots while older specimens take on a more bronze or copper color with smaller but more numerous spots. This makes young Sertanejo plecos look very similar to snowball plecos with the most obvious difference between the two being their dentition. Being a voracious herbivore, they can make a great candidate for algae maintenance in cooler South American community tanks.

products.title: Red Leporinus
products.body_html:
Remarks: The red leporinus is native to Bahia, Brazil, and is rare within the hobby. Therefore, not much is known about it, especially in regards to care. It is likely they behave similar to other species of leporinus in that they can be nippy toward fish with long fins and are best kept in groups of 5 or more.

  Red leporinus have silver or gold bodies with three black spots along their lateral line, a larger one under the dorsal fin, a mid-sized one under the adipose fin, and then the smallest one right at the base of the caudal fin. When in the right conditions, their fins are a nice reddish-orange color that stands out nicely against a black background.

products.title: Red Calico Platy
products.body_html: Remarks: A perfect starter fish! Platies are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. Red calico platies have reddish-orange bodies with black dots and patches.

products.title: Red Striped Eartheater
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The red striped eartheater is a rare, close relative of Geophagus sveni and looks very similar to them as well as a number of other species of Geophagus. This has resulted in them frequently getting misconstrued with one another. According to taxonomic literature, adult specimens can be identified by having four, faint, vertical, parallel bars on the body when stressed or deceased. Hobbyists have also claimed they can be identified by these fish having stripes rather than spots in their caudal fin, no dark markings on the head, and the black spot on the lateral line always being present even if just faintly. Similar to G. sveni, the red striped eartheater is a slow grower but makes it worth the wait in the end. As an adult and if in the right conditions, they display bright blue and red horizontal stripes across their bodies accented by a golden sheen. This combined with the elegant streamers the males develop results in an absolutely beautiful looking fish.

  Like all Geophagus, red striped eartheaters require a sandy substrate due to their earth-eating habits. They sift mouthfuls of sand through their gills, pulling out bits of food to eat. Gravel presents a very high probability of choking or refusing to eat. Also like their close relatives, these fish do best in groups of 4 or more but can also do well in single male and female pairs. Due to their large size, adult red striped eartheaters should be kept in an aquarium that is at least 90 gallons if kept as a single, breeding pair, but 125 gallons or more is needed if they are to be kept with other fish. When spawning, they can become territorial as they are protective parents and they have a good chance of swallowing tank mates that can fit in their mouths. These behaviors should be taken into consideration when planning a tank for these wonderful fish.

  We imported these fish as Geophagus surinamensis but it is very rare to actually get the true species due to how uncommon they are and the fact they look similar to many other species of Geophagus when young and even as adults. Due to this, we do not feel it is right to guarantee these are for sure Geophagus surinamensis  at their current size. Therefore, there is NO GUARANTEE these are this exact species.

products.title: Longtail Spatula Whiptail
products.body_html:
Remarks: The longtail spatula whiptail is a very large, uncommon Loricariid mainly found in the Rio Orteguasa in Columbia. However, there are reports of it also being found in the Rio Napo in both Ecuador and Peru. Within both rivers, they inhabit spaces with very clear, fast-flowing waters rich in dissolved oxygen and almost devoid of pollutants such as nitrates. These conditions should be simulated within the aquarium with plenty of aeration and small, frequent water changes to ensure the longtail spatula whiptail remains healthy. 

  While this species has fairly mundane coloration of a light brown or tan body with about 4 wide, dark brown bands down the tail and one behind the head, its size, shape, and behavior are very attractive. Like all whiptails, its body is fairly thin and flat but elongated resulting in a very unique shape. Even more interesting is that it possesses very long streamers extending from the tips of its caudal fin that looks very graceful as it swims about the tank. Unlike many of its cousins, the longtail spatula whiptail is not very shy and will happily swim about the tank during the day as long as it is maintained in the appropriate conditions. This active behavior pair with its territorial habits toward conspecifics makes it where this fish should be maintained in a tank with a length of at least 5 ft to give it ample swimming space. A standard 125 gallon typically will be sufficient for 2-3 of these whiptails. All of this makes this species perfect for those looking for a very large whiptail that won't just hide away all day.

products.title: Sanke Koi Swordtail
products.body_html:
Remarks: Sanke koi swordtails possess mostly white or silver bodies with patches of reddish-orange and black speckles throughout. This goes in line with their name as "sanke" means "tricolor." This color variation and high contrast results in a striking fish that stands out well in just about any aquarium.

  Swordtails are peaceful community fish that reproduce with ease. They are livebearers, meaning the females release live fry rather than laying eggs. It is recommended to keep more females than males, as males can easily harass females with relentless attempts to reproduce, leading to stress in the females. Dominant males will begin developing swords and a modified anal fin, called a gonopodium, around 1-1.5" in length. However, it is not uncommon for less dominant males to maintain the physical appearance of a female until much later in life (even as large as 4"!) Some have also reported the strange phenomenon of these fish occasionally changing gender with it typically occurring with older, large females becoming male with mixed results in regards to them remaining fertile.

products.title: Black Saddled Hoplisoma
products.body_html:
Remarks:   The black saddled Hoplisoma, also known as the new panda Hoplisoma, is a medium-sized Corydoradinae that is found in a small creek near the settlement of Villa Bittencourt which lies on the border between Colombia, Brazil and Peru. A very similar, close relative known as the black diamond Hoplisoma can also be found here with both species sometimes being found intermingling among the fallen branches in the tannin-stained water. Both have white or tan bodies with a black mask over the eyes with the main difference between the two being the shape of the large black spot that runs from their lateral line above the pectoral fin into the dorsal fin. The black diamond Hoplisoma has a diamond shaped spot, while the black saddled Hoplisoma has a more rounded, almost semi-circle shape.

  Black saddled Hoplisomas were previously classified as Corydoras sp. CW051, but were reclassified as Hoplisoma sp. CW051 upon publication of a phylogenomic analysis in 2024 that reclassified many species in the Corydoradinae subfamily.

  Corydoradinae are small, peaceful catfish that are active shoalers and will stay near conspecifics, making for a lovely sight. They feed by searching through sand with their barbels, making such a substrate necessary for their long-term health. Gravel substrates tend to be abrasive and damage the barbels, leading to infections and an inability to find food.

  While these friendly catfish are willing to swim with other species of Corydoradinae different from their own, this does not provide the same social need as being kept with members of their own species. Because of this, it is highly recommended to keep at least six of each species of Corydoradinae present in the aquarium and only mix species if one has the space to keep at least six of each.

  Corydoradinae are often looked at as "scavengers," but they should be treated in a similar fashion as the rest of your fish. They are omnivores and require a mixed diet. Sinking prepared foods are readily accepted, as are frozen and live foods such as brine shrimp, blackworms, and bloodworms.

products.title: Inka Apistogramma
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The inka apistogramma is a smaller, but very attractive species of dwarf cichlid from Peru. This species is uncommon and was described fairly recently back in 2004 after being discovered in Peru in fairly cool water compared to the habitats of other apistogramma species. This combined with their tolerance of more neutral water parameters has resulted in them being a fairly easy to keep dwarf cichlid. While they tend to form monogamous pairs, these fish do best when kept in pairs or groups of five or more to spread out any territorial conflict.

  Similar to Apistogramma cacatuoides, male inka apistogrammas get tall dorsal fin spines creating a sort of "mohawk" look when mature. Males also possess yellow faces and fins, blue bodies, and a reddish-orange crescent on the caudal fin. Females show the typical golden yellow coloration, dark black ventral fins with orange tips, and black vertical bars that continue up into the dorsal fin resulting in them being equally as attractive as the males. Both males and females can show a black mask as well as a black dot at the base of the tail.

  The tank should be furnished with several caves or nooks to protect and spawn in with cichlid caves, overturned flower pots, and leaf litter working well for this purpose.  Breeding is achieved by feeding live baby brine shrimp to an adult pair and providing a proper tank setup. Fry are guarded by one or both parents for several weeks. Dither fish such as small tetras and livebearers can be used to provide parents with targets to chase.

products.title: Red Root Floater
products.body_html: Remarks: Red root floaters are probably one of the most desirable floating plants in the hobby. They look like small clusters of lily pads with short, feathery, bright red roots that hang below the leaves. Typically this plant has green leaves, but under high light conditions, they can turn a deep crimson color just like their roots and will even produce tiny, white flowers. While red root floaters enjoy high nutrient environments, having lower amounts of nitrates in the water can result in redder leaves. Like all floaters, these plants will not survive if they are constantly getting water on top of them so the surface of the water should be as calm as possible. This makes them an excellent addition to calmer tanks like those with bettas.

products.title: Dolphin Manda
products.body_html: Remarks: The dolphin manda is an uncommon mbuna that is also known as the giant demasoni. This is due to the fact that this species looks very similar to the smaller demasoni mbuna with the same black and blue striped pattern with yellow egg spots on the males. The dolphin manda is a rather large mbuna, with males commonly topping out at 6" long, but have a surprisingly peaceful disposition for an mbuna. This makes them one of the few that can do well with peacock cichlids, even in the presence of females. Provide these fish with plenty of room to swim along with an herbivorous diet and they will make an excellent addition to a more peaceful Lake Malawi community tank.

products.title: Süsswassertang
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Süsswassertang is a true oddball of an aquarium plant. It grows similarly to a moss in that it floats loosely or attaches to structures. It also has a simple structure similar to seaweed, something alluded to in its German name, Süßwassertang, which means "freshwater seaweed." Its leafy and homogenous appearance has led many to mistake it as a liverwort, but despite all of this, it is actually a type of fern according to genetic testing. It was discovered back in 2001 by botanist, Christel Kasselmann, who promptly introduced it into the aquarium hobby where it became highly desirable due to its easy care and unique appearance.

  In the aquarium, Süsswassertang can be grown in a variety of ways, growing well attached to surfaces, rolled into balls, floating, or even spread out across the substrate loosely as a sort of carpet. It can adapt to almost any water parameters typically found in aquaria and will be just fine in a low light environment, but it will explode in growth if placed under high light. Propagation is achieved by simply tearing off pieces of the main plant which will grow into a new plant. 

If you are only ordering plants, you are eligible for a shipping rate of $19.99. Please include a note or email requesting a partial shipping refund when you order.

products.title: San Juan Gastrodermus
products.body_html:
Remarks:  Not much is known about the San Juan Gastrodermus as it is rather new to the hobby. They are known to be found in Bolivia, Peru, and Brazil most likely in areas with lots of fallen branches for them to hide under. The San Juan Gastrodermus looks very similar to the little Gastrodermus (Gastrodermus nanus) with thin tan, white, brown, and black horizontal stripes across their rounded bodies. These Gastrodermus are rather interesting to watch as they hover under structures and within the water column, rather than spend all of their time on the bottom, similar to the behavior of pygmy Gastrodermus. It was previously classified as 𝐶𝑜𝑟𝑦𝑑𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑠 bilineatus, but was reclassified as Gastrodermus bilineatus upon publication of a phylogenomic analysis in 2024 that reclassified many species in the Corydoradinae subfamily.

  Corydoradinae are small, peaceful catfish that are active shoalers and will stay near conspecifics, making for a lovely sight. They feed by searching through sand with their barbels, making such a substrate necessary for their long-term health. Gravel substrates tend to be abrasive and damage the barbels, leading to infections and an inability to find food.

  While these friendly catfish are willing to swim with other species of Corydoradinae different from their own, this does not provide the same social need as being kept with members of their own species. Because of this, it is highly recommended to keep at least six of each species of Corydoradinae present in the aquarium and only mix species if one has the space to keep at least six of each.

  Corydoradinae are often looked at as "scavengers," but they should be treated in a similar fashion as the rest of your fish. They are omnivores and require a mixed diet. Sinking prepared foods are readily accepted, as are frozen and live foods such as brine shrimp, blackworms, and bloodworms.

products.title: Leopold's Angelfish
products.body_html:
Remarks:  The Leopold's angelfish is the third accepted species in the Pterophyllum genus and can be found in both northern Brazil and southern Guyana. There, they can be found in slow moving, acidic tributaries under floating plants and wood. These fish are commonly misnamed as Pterophyllum dumerilii though the accepted scientific name is Pterophyllum leopoldi. One of it's common names, the Roman-nosed angelfish, comes from an identifying feature of this species where the face has a more arched slope and a longer mouth. This shape is not always easy to identify when the fish is young, so another aspect to look for is the presence of a black blotch just under the beginning of the dorsal fin. Upon maturity and when in the right conditions, the Leopold's angelfish display a bright blue, iridescent spot on its gill plate just behind the eye and also possesses red fins that stand out nicely against their muted yellow bodies.

  While this species is intolerant in regards to high pH or hardness, it is more tolerant in regards to required space compared to its relatives with 30 gallons being enough for a pair. This is due to the fact that the Leopold's angelfish is the smallest of the Pterophyllum genus, typically growing only to about four inches in length and six inches in height. Being smaller makes it an even better candidate for a peaceful South American tank compared to scalare or altum. Despite all these great qualities, the Leopold's angelfish is rarely imported or bred in captivity making it very uncommon in the hobby.

  Angelfish do best when housed in South American community setups. Pairs may act aggressively when spawning, but the species is typically peaceful overall. Provide them with plenty of tall decorations such as driftwood and sword plants to swim through and use as spawning surfaces.

products.title: Diamond Apistogramma
products.body_html:
Remarks: The diamond apistogramma is a small species of apisto native to tributaries flowing into the Rio Itaya in Peru. There they hide among leaf litter and other botanicals in areas of soft, acidic, tannin-stained, and calm water. Both their common and scientific name comes from the identifying, roughly diamond-shaped black spot on the base of the tail. This species can be rather cryptic especially if kept under bright lights, so they will do best if kept in dim lighting with calm tankmates that won't frighten them. A sandy substrate is necessary due to their earth-eating habits.

  When mature and in the right conditions, male diamond apistogrammas display blue spotting on a red gill plate, blue fins, and an impressively tall, mohawk-like, dorsal fin. Females show the usual bright yellow with dark black markings. The tank should be furnished with several caves or nooks to protect and spawn in with cichlid caves, overturned flower pots, and leaf litter working well for this purpose.  Breeding is achieved by feeding live baby brine shrimp to an adult pair and providing a proper tank setup. Fry are guarded by one or both parents for several weeks. Dither fish such as small tetras and livebearers can be used to provide parents with targets to chase.

  Care for them properly and you will be rewarded with a fish that displays beautiful coloration, unique fin shape, and fascinating parental care as is typical for apistogrammas.

products.title: Tefe Green Discus
products.body_html:
Remarks: The Tefe green discus is one of the many striking, wild discus varieties found in Peru in both the Western Amazon Basin and in Lake Tefe where they get their common name. Upon maturity and if in the right conditions, they will display a bright orange body with darker orange speckles. They also have metallic turquoise striations on the face, upper, and lower body that make them really stand out against a black background and in blackwater set ups.

  Discus are often referred to as the king of aquarium fish. This nickname arose from the sheer beauty of these fish along with their reputation for being difficult to care for making those who keep them successfully revered within the hobby. It being difficult to keep discus was an opinion developed when majority of specimens available were wild caught. With most now being captive bred and even line bred to produce many different color variants such as turquoise and pigeon blood, they are much more hardy. That being said, discus still require very clean, soft, acidic water with little to no nitrates and warm temperatures.

  These peaceful cichlids tend to be timid, especially when it comes to feeding, so they shouldn't be kept with overly boisterous or aggressive fish. Typically their mouths are very small, but once they reach adulthood they are capable of swallowing very tiny fish so this should also be considered when choosing tankmates. If attempting to breed discus, lots of vertical surfaces should be provided for them to lay their eggs on and it should be noted they can become territorial when spawning. Once the fry hatch and are free-swimming, both parents show an incredible form of parental care in which they feed their fry by allowing them to eat their slime coat. This behavior is an amazing sight and makes breeding discus very enjoyable.

products.title: Heckel Discus
products.body_html:
Remarks:   Heckel discus are one of the most colorful, challenging to keep, and oldest known wild discus variants. They display vibrant yellows, oranges, greens, and blues that vary in amount and pattern depending on the exact locality they are sourced from. Typically Heckel discus are identified by the particularly wide and dark band that runs down the center of their body that is particularly round even for a discus. Care should be taken in regards to water parameters as these discus are wild and much less tolerant of incorrect water parameters. For the best chance at being successful, keep them in very soft, acidic, warm water and perform frequent water changes. 

  Discus are often referred to as the king of aquarium fish. This nickname arose from the sheer beauty of these fish along with their reputation for being difficult to care for making those who keep them successfully revered within the hobby. It being difficult to keep discus was an opinion developed when majority of specimens available were wild caught.
